The amount of changes diff-shades reports for this PR scares me, especially since I'm not entirely sure if it's really an improvement in situations like these:
```diff
--- a/warehouse:warehouse/packaging/services.py
+++ b/warehouse:warehouse/packaging/services.py
@@ -33,13 +33,15 @@
         # This class should not be used in production, it's trivial for it to
         # be used to read arbitrary files from the disk. It is intended ONLY
         # for local development with trusted users. To make this clear, we'll
         # raise a warning.
         warnings.warn(
-            "LocalFileStorage is intended only for use in development, you "
-            "should not use it in production due to the lack of safe guards "
-            "for safely locating files on disk.",
+            (
+                "LocalFileStorage is intended only for use in development, you "
+                "should not use it in production due to the lack of safe guards "
+                "for safely locating files on disk."
+            ),
             InsecureStorageWarning,
         )
```
To be fair, the extra parenthesises and indention are mostly fine, but seem redundant. Probably doesn't make much sense to add even more complexity to the "certain contexts" part of this formatting rule though to keep stuff like this unchanged. Checking whether the string's previous 
(previous, cause the comma) or next (next) sibling is also a string sounds incredibly fragile.

The two conventions for module-level variables I see in pep8 are:

[Global variable names](https://peps.python.org/pep-0008/#global-variable-names):

> (Let’s hope that these variables are meant for use inside one module only.) The conventions are about the same as those for functions.

> Modules that are designed for use via from M import * should use the __all__ mechanism to prevent exporting globals, or use the older convention of prefixing such globals with an underscore (which you might want to do to indicate these globals are “module non-public”).

and [Constants](https://peps.python.org/pep-0008/#constants):

> Constants are usually defined on a module level and written in all capital letters with underscores separating words. Examples include MAX_OVERFLOW and TOTAL.

This seems fine and the additional io manager seems necessary to avoid switching to using in-memory when using the in-process executor. Ideally, some of the same improvements you've added to `default_job_io_manager_with_fs_io_manager_schema` could be added to the normal default job IO manager, but that can be a separate PR certainly. 

We originally kept using `default_job_io_manager` for the job case to easily power `materialize_to_memory` and testing IIRC, but I'm realizing that it also introduces a weird behavior disparity, which I think was an oversight, for jobs that are created using `define_asset_job` when assets are provided resource or not. 
- If I call define_asset_job and I used `with_resources` on my assets, the default IO manager is `default_job_io_manager_with_fs_io_manager_schema`.
- If I call define_asset_job and did not use `with_resources` on my assets, the default IO manager is `default_job_io_manager`. 
- Perhaps most pressingly (and maybe write a unit test to show this is the case), I think that if you call with_resources on only some of your assets and not others, and then create a job using all of them, it will simply fail to compile because of different resources for the io manager key.

Solving this is probably a breaking change, but should get something sensible here for 1.1. Fine with approving, since this behavior disparity predates this PR, but should consider ways to fix.

Great start! Super clean, clever code with good practices! This will be a very appreciated feature. Just to know for another time, before we start looking at PRs they should normally:
- have a CHANGELOG.md entry
- pass tests; the one that is currently failing illustrates an issue!
- introduce new tests if the PR adds a new feature, or otherwise more than a non-negligible number of lines of moving parts
- ideally name the branches without '/' as the branch names get reused for Docker image names, and it can be a pain to get Podman to pull the right image with certain special characters in the name.

There is one major issue with the logic still. It does not consider score categories with a `category_aggregation = sum` properly. These need sums of max and min values for all predictors within the category. Consider `protein_prediction` in the demo case: there two protein predictors in the 1.20 model, Sift and Polyphen. Each of those can give one point to the score, so e.g. http://localhost:5000/cust000/643594/4c7d5c70d955875504db72ef8e1abe77 gets score 2 but the range is shown as 0<x<1.

Thanks for your contribution. Your idea is cool but the word "hook" is too ambiguous here.

Correct me if I am wrong. We originally support customized key generating method (reducer and splitter), but the values cannot be changed. Therefore, you are proposing this hook for changing the values.

I think this is not related to the flatten or unflatten process, but might be good to have for saving memory usage or computing time. We can do all the things we want in one dict creation process instead of having some intermediate dict and doing some post processing. If this is the purpose, and think there are some other ways that are more flexible and pythonic.

For example, we can make a generator `Flatten.items()`:
```
{key: hook(key, value) for key, value in Flatten(d).items()}
```
I think this will make the code much more readable. I'm actually planning to make a Flatten class like this, so please tell me your suggestion.

Doesn't the `*Scheduler` already completely determine the makeup of the batches?

I seems to me that there is a lot of machinery to make sure an epoch has a defined length. Do we lose a lot if we just say "If you're doing multi-task learning, all iterators cycle ad infinitum, and you have to specify `instances_per_epoch` all the time?

The way it is now, the shortest iterator determines the epoch length, though not exactly, since it depends on what the scheduler does. When you specify `instances_per_epoch`, the story is different though, and now it's determined by the sampler, where you can only change things at the beginning of the epoch.

What's supposed to happen if the sampler says we should use only `"task_a"`, and the scheduler only schedules instances from `"task_b"`? What will happen in this code is that the epoch ends up with 0 instances, which will be surprising. The problem is that I can specify that preference in two places, and the two places can contradict each other.

The concept of an "epoch" as the complete training set doesn't really make sense in this setting. It only works out that way in a few special cases. I don't think we have to go so far out of our way to preserve it.

I still think this PR requires some significant changes, however. I left some comments on specific files, but still have 2 high-level concerns that haven't been addressed:

1. I strongly believe passing positions via file should flow through a configuration value like `positions_file` or something, instead of assuming a specific naming convention and (in my opinion, unnecessarily) having the restriction that you can only use positions from a file if your training data is also in a file.

2. I do believe that the interface should be that users provider integers, not strings of the form `pos_{i}`. To be honest @metpavel , I did not understand some of the things you said in https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/5929#issuecomment-1633729902.

For example, how is it that asking someone to provide `pos_1, pos_2, ...` is somehow avoiding the need to "perform the mapping to integers beforehand"?

And how does that string contract make it "easier for a user to specify a position of an item in complex layouts (let's say 2D)"?

Even beyond these things... integers will be easier for users to produce (no need to append this special `"pos_"` prefix and cast to string), and will result in smaller file sizes (which might be significant for large datasets).

General comment is that we should figure out what the target types are going to be and which goals map to them. Otherwise introducing `rust_crate` here as the focus for `fmt` might need to change in the future.

I was envisioning target types inspired by the Go backend:
- `rust_package` maps to `go_mod`

This looks good overall. Regarding the ManagedArray discussion, the thing to keep in mind is that conceptually `ManagedArray` is really a low-level RAII wrapper around a pointer to a contiguous memory buffer whose ownership can be transferred. `std::vector` should be used more like a list, whereas `ManagedArray` should be like a numpy array. The expectation is that Python users acquiring `ManagedArray` variables expect that those variables should be zero-copy but remain unchanged if a recompute is triggered. Since it looks like you're replacing all the `ManagedArray` data in the vector on recompute, I think you'll be fine. I'll double-check this on my final pass though since I was a bit cursory with checking that this time around.

> I think **Split in this class** is not a good way because we need to load the whole data to get whether train data or test data.

Not sure I understand. You are already loading the complete file, so what is difference? Plus, we are talking about ~800MB here which is not terribly large.

My suggestion is to have two parameters: `split: Optional[str] = None` and `split_ratio: int = 10`.

1. For `split=None`, we return the full `data` without any split on our side. Meaning, we ignore `split_ratio`.
2. For `split="train"`, we return `data[:split_ratio]`.
3. For `split="test"`, we return `data[split_ratio:]`.

Here `data` is a single video in the dataset, i.e. `(T, C, H, W)`. With this the user by default gets the unsupervised data by default, but has convenient access to the splits defined by the authors.

In addition, the user can set the `split_ratio` to their liking. IMO that is enough for the first round. 

Generating new data is somewhat unrelated to `MovingMNIST` since they don't do anything special other than bouncing data in a larger image. Tensorflow has this functionality, but in a general way. If someone needs it, we can always add that later.

Thoughts?

Some quick thoughts.

This looks inspired by the `HelmCluster` which is a great starting point but a slightly different paradigm. In `HelmCluster` we are always connecting to an existing cluster, because they can only be created by `helm`.

In this cluster manager we want two different ways of creating things, one where we create the cluster, and another where we connect to an existing cluster.

I would expect if someone did `cluster = KubeCluster2(name="foo", ...)` it will create a `DaskCluster` resource called `foo` via the Kubernetes API, wait for the service to become available and then connect an RPC to it. This should fail if the cluster already exists.

I would also expect folks to be able to run `cluster = KubeCluster2.from_name("foo")` which skips creating the resources and just connects to the existing cluster. This should fail if this doesn't already exist.

First off, thank you for this work. There are some considerable gems in here and I am appreciating the conversation this initiates. As for my review, I am finding it difficult to focus on the large number of actual proposed changes in light of the equally large number of formatting changes. To help me get traction, I will focus on commenting on the high level changes I see proposed here:

1. Moving managing Order over to the OrdersClient does make sense from a separation of concerns point of view.
2. I would further advocate for managing (paged)Orders to the OrdersClient as well, and possibly spending some time on design of a `pager` that, similar to the `waiter`, can be abstracted across all clients (as aws does)
3. I am curious why you are advocating for having `models` manage the Order dataclass. Could OrdersClient do the same just as well? While it does seem nice to have one 'go to' for a data structure (aka `models` here), it seems to tightly couple OrdersClient to models, as evidenced by the `orders` module importing from `models` and the `models` tests import from the `orders` module.
4. Regarding the proposal to use JSON as IO in the CLI and data structures as IO for the Python library, I think this is an excellent discussion point and may be evidence that we could be well served by going through the Python library public interface, similar to what we've done for the CLI, and coming to a consensus.

Thanks, the migration towards standard JSONField is certainly a good thing to do. 

The migration can't be done by just changing the existing field type, the database needs to be migrated as well. This is typically done by constructing temporary real JSONField, inserting old content there, removing the old one and renaming the temporary one. The tricky part is how to do this properly in a library while keeping interchangeable type for JSON field.

For my understanding, in the flow from the network through Plasma to building the DataFrame, where was the large memory inflation happening? My understanding of the flow is:
1. Plasma buffers are allocated and filled as objects are pulled, with the Arrow tables being deserialized directly into the Plasma buffers.
2. The block builder holds pointers to those Plasma buffers, so no new copies are made while add blocks to the builder.
3. The `.build()` and `.to_pandas()` calls create a copy or two during the Arrow table concatenation and the DataFrame construction, respectively.

This PR deals with stages (1) and (2) rather than the copy-heavy stage (3), and AFAICT wouldn't result in a lower peak during those two stages. Am I missing the creation of an extra copy or two in (1) and (2), maybe receiver-side queueing of byte chunks during pulls, or something else?

I'm reading https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/stable.html. It poses some important considerations and suggests to test all minor Python versions. According to psutil's `setup.py` the most minor version we support is Python 3.4:

```python
    if setuptools is not None:
        kwargs.update(
            python_requires=">=2.7, !=3.0.*, !=3.1.*, !=3.2.*, !=3.3.*",
            extras_require=extras_require,
            zip_safe=False,
        )
```

As such I wonder: 
* would it make sense for `build.yml` to test 3.4? 
* what python versions are we testing right now?  I see that windows has `["3.7", "3.8", "3.9", "3.10", "~3.11.0-0"]` but I'm unclear about the other platforms.
